items.title: Startup using GitHub Flow
items.scenario: A 6-person team ships a SaaS web app multiple times per day.
items.explanation: Each ticket → feature branch → PR → 1 review + green CI → squash-merge to `main` → auto-deploy. No `develop`, no `release/*` — `main` is the source of truth.

items.title: Library maintainer using GitFlow
items.scenario: An open-source Python library with quarterly minor releases and monthly patch releases.
items.explanation: Features land in `develop`. When ready, cut `release/1.4.0`, run final QA, merge to `main`, tag `v1.4.0`. A bug in `v1.3.x` becomes `hotfix/1.3.1` off `main`, then back-merged to `develop`.

items.title: Gen AI eval branch hygiene
items.scenario: An ML engineer tests three reranker strategies in parallel.
items.explanation: Three `experiment/rerank-*` branches off `main`. Each runs the eval suite. The winner becomes `feature/rerank-cross-encoder` and goes through the normal PR flow.

items.title: Trunk-based with feature flags
items.scenario: A team wants to merge an unfinished LLM-judge eval to `main` without exposing it.
items.explanation: The code goes behind `if cfg.experimental.llm_judge:`. The flag stays off in production until the feature is ready. `main` remains deployable; collaborators can iterate without long branches.

items.title: Environment branches for staged rollout
items.scenario: GitLab Flow with `main → pre-production → production` branches.
items.explanation: Merges to `main` deploy to staging automatically. Promotion to prod is `merge main → pre-production` (after smoke tests) and then `merge pre-production → production` (after sign-off).
